results.title: Decoding typed sentences from non-invasive brain activity
results.content:
Brain2Qwerty v2 overcomes this limit and generates the sentences directly from a continuous recording of brain activity.
This new model now combines three hierarchical modules to jointly improve the decoding of letters, words, and sentences.

MEG signal feeds a Conformer (character detection), then an Aligner (word embeddings), then an LLM that reconstructs the sentence: the robot moves very fast.
MEG signal to Conformer to Aligner to LLM to predicted sentence.

Performance

Trained on 10× more data per participant, Brain2Qwerty v2 can decode complete and meaningful sentences solely from MEG signals of healthy volunteers, and reaches up to 78% word accuracy for the best participant.

Explore results [...] # Brain2Qwertyv2

A non-invasive brain-computer interface for online sentence generation.

The Challenge

Every year, thousands of people lose the ability to speak after a stroke, an accident, or a brain disorder. While communication can be restored with brain implants on the motor cortex, such neuroprostheses require open-brain surgery.

Today, we introduce Brain2Qwerty v2: a model to decode natural sentences from non-invasive magnetoencephalography (MEG) recordings.

Architecture

For this, we built off the Brain2Qwerty v1 architecture released last year and recently accepted at Nature Neuroscience. Brain2Qwerty v1 consisted of predicting keystrokes from MEG brain activity patterns recorded at BCBL, but could not work in real time because it needed the timing of every keypress.

results.title: Accurate Decoding of Natural Sentences from Non- ...
results.content:
1 2 9 3 Discussion Summary.
Brain2Qwerty v2 can accurately decode sentences solely from continuous non-invasive brain recordings by jointly training three deep learning modules, focusing on decoding character, word, and sentence-level representations, respectively. This model achieves WER of 39% on average across subjects, and 22% WER for our best subject. This is a twofold improvement over the previous state-of-the art (52% for the best subject in Brain2Qwerty v1 (Lévy et al., 2025)) significantly narrowing the gap with invasive brain-to-text decoders (Willett et al., 2021; Metzger et al., 2023).
Semantic decoding. [...] supervised this work Restoring communication for people who have lost the ability to speak or move after a brain injury is a major challenge. While intracranial implants now enable high-performing brain-computer-interfaces, non-invasive alternatives are still lagging behind. Here, we present Brain2Qwerty v2, a model that can decode the production of natural sentences solely from real-time magnetoencephalography (MEG) recordings. By collecting 22,000 sentences typed by nine subjects, each recorded for 10 hours, our model leverages character, word and sentence-level representations to achieve an average word error rate (WER) of 39%. For our best participant, the model accurately decodes half of the sentences with one word error or less. Critically, decoding accuracy log-linearly improves [...] To address these three challenges, we introduce Brain2Qwerty v2, a deep learning framework designed to decode a 22,000-sentence corpus from non-invasive magnetoencephalographic (MEG) recordings. We trained 2 the model on a delayed-typing task performed by nine healthy volunteers across 90 total recording sessions. In each trial, participants listened to a sentence via headphones, waited through a forced delay, and then typed the corresponding text. Our decoding study focuses on the neural activity during the language production phase. This new dataset presents 10 times more data per subject than Lévy et al. (2025) and spans a much larger diversity of sentences (Figure 1C). Building on this dataset, our approach leverages AI in three main ways: First, we resolve the real-time challenge

results.title: Meta Brain2Qwerty v2: Reading Your Thoughts Without Surgery
results.content:
People with ALS, locked-in syndrome, or anarthria lose the ability to speak or move — but their brains keep generating language. Invasive brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) have restored communication for some patients, at the cost of neurosurgery, infection risk, and long-term implant maintenance. For years, non-invasive alternatives have lagged far behind.

On June 25, 2026, Meta FAIR published Brain2Qwerty v2, a model that decodes full typed sentences solely from magnetoencephalography (MEG) signals — no implants, no surgery. The average word error rate across 9 participants: 39%. The best participant: 22% WER, with 47% of sentences decoded within one word of the target. [...] Non-invasive alternatives like EEG suffer from poor signal-to-noise ratio. MEG offers better temporal resolution, but until Brain2Qwerty, decoding full sentences from it was considered too hard. The v1 paper (Lévy et al., 2025) achieved 32% character error rate (CER) — but it required knowing the exact timing of each keystroke in advance, making real-time use impossible.

Brain2Qwerty v2 removes all of these constraints at once.

## How Brain2Qwerty v2 Works

The pipeline has three jointly trained components, each handling a different level of linguistic abstraction.

### 1. The Encoder: Asynchronous Character Decoding via CTC [...] explainx.ainewsletter3.4k

# Meta Brain2Qwerty v2: Reading Your Thoughts Without Surgery

Meta FAIR's Brain2Qwerty v2 decodes full sentences from non-invasive MEG at 39% average WER — 22% for the best participant. No implants, no surgery.

·13 min read·Yash Thakker

results.title: Brain2Qwerty V2 Advances Non-Invasive Brain-To-Text AI ...
results.content:
The Brain2Qwerty v2 approach uses magnetoencephalography, or MEG, to record brain activity externally. Researchers trained the system on roughly 22,000 sentences from nine volunteer participants. Each participant wore an MEG device for 10 hours while actively typing, creating paired data between neural signals and written language.

Rather than relying on manually engineered steps to detect specific neural events, Brain2Qwerty v2 uses end-to-end deep learning to decode directly from raw brain signals. This design matters because non-invasive brain recordings are noisy, complex, and difficult to interpret. By training the model on raw signal patterns, the pipeline can learn useful representations without requiring a rigid hand-built decoding process.

### How LLMs play a role [...] Can AI turn brain activity into words without surgery? Brain2Qwerty v2 suggests the answer is moving closer to yes. The new... Brain2Qwerty V2 Advances Non-Invasive Brain-To-Text AI Decoding

Can AI turn brain activity into words without surgery? Brain2Qwerty v2 suggests the answer is moving closer to yes. The new research presents a non-invasive, end-to-end AI pipeline that decodes sentences from brain recordings in real time, reaching accuracy levels that were previously associated mainly with invasive brain-computer interface techniques. [...] ### How LLMs play a role

Large language models also play a key role. Fine-tuning language models on neural data helps the system use semantic context when converting noisy brain signals into coherent text. In practice, this means the model does not only map signal fragments to isolated words. It can use the surrounding language structure to improve sentence-level decoding.

The results show a major improvement over prior non-invasive methods. Brain2Qwerty v2 achieved a 61% word accuracy rate, compared with 8% word accuracy reported for other non-invasive approaches. The best-performing participant reached 78% word accuracy, with more than half of decoded sentences containing one word error or fewer.

results.title: From Brain Waves to Words: Brain2Qwerty Offers a New Path to ...
results.content:
June 29, 2026•

3 minute read

Video 1

Last year, we introduced Brain2Qwerty v1, research that uses AI to decode brain activity into text without any surgical implant. Now we're sharing the next step: Brain2Qwerty v2, the highest-performing end-to-end pipeline capable of real-time sentence decoding from non-invasive brain recordings, approaching levels of accuracy previously exclusive to techniques that require brain surgery. [...] Image 2

We trained Brain2Qwerty v2 on approximately 22,000 sentences from nine volunteer participants, each recorded for 10 hours wearing a magnetoencephalography (MEG) device while actively typing. Instead of relying on hand-crafted pipelines to detect neural events, we use end-to-end deep learning to decode directly from raw brain signals.

Image 3

Fine-tuning large language models on neural data allows the system to leverage semantic context, bridging the gap between noisy brain recordings and coherent language. We also deployed AI agents to explore optimizations for the decoding pipeline, with final training configurations selected manually by engineers. [...] Image 4
The result: Brain2Qwerty v2 recovers sentences coherently from noisy neural inputs, achieving a word accuracy rate of 61%, significantly improving upon the 8% word accuracy from other non-invasive methods). And for our best participant, we achieve a 78% word accuracy, where more than half of all sentences are decoded with one word error or less.
